semanticCore: Rescue, saving, delivering, extraction, or recovery
quranicRelevance: Rescuing/saving from Hellfire (Anqatha - 'fa-anqathakum minha'), and inability of idols to rescue their food from flies ('la yastanqithoohu').

keyDerivations.meaning: To rescue, save, deliver, or pull out of danger
To extract, rescue back, recover, or deliver from custody

figurativeOrRareUsages: وَإِنْ يَسْلُبْهُمُ الذُّبَابُ شَيْئًا لَا يَسْتَنْقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ (23:73): And if the fly should snatch anything from them, they cannot rescue/recover it back from it (utter weakness of idols).
discardedNoise: Modern rescue boats (Anqath) at sea beaches.
summaryEn: The root denotes rescue, detailing the divine saving (Inqadh) of Arab tribes from pre-Islamic hellish wars, and the helpless idols unable to rescue (Yastanqithoo) food from flies.
summaryAr: يدل هذا الجذر على الإنقاذ والتخليص من الهلكة، واصفاً إنقاذ الأمة من حفرة النار الحربية الجاهلية (فأنقذكم)، وعجز الآلهة الباطلة عن استرداد سلب الذباب.
rawText: إِسْتَنْقَذَ 10 see 4. الأَنْقَذُ [or أَنْقَذُ, without the art.,] The hedgehog; القُتْفُذُ: (K;) as also with د. (TA.) انقذهُ 4 , (S, A, L, Msb, K,) inf. n. إِنْقَاذٌ; (K;) and 🡻استنقذهُ; and 🡻تنقّذهُ; (S, A, L, K;) and 🡻نقّذٌ, inf. n. تَنْقِيذٌ; and 🡻نَقَذَهُ, [aor. ﹹ,] inf. n. نَقْذٌ; (K;) He saved, rescued, or liberated, him or it, (S, A, L, Msb, K,) from such a one, (S, L,) and from evil. (Msb.) تَنَقَّذَ 5 see 4. نَقَذٌ A thing that one has saved, rescued, or liberated; (S, L, Msb, K;) of the measure فَعَلٌ in the sense of the measure مَفْعُولٌ, like نَفَضٌ and قَبَضٌ; (S, L;) as also 🡻نَقِيذٌ and نَقِيذَةٌ: (L:) a man saved, or rescued, or liberated: (L:) also, a horse taken from another people: (L:) or a camel &c. taken by, and then recovered from, the enemy; as also نَقِيذَةٌ and نَقِيذٌ; pl. نَقَائِذُ: (A:) or 🡻نَقِيذَةٌ signifies a horse saved, or rescued, or liberated, (S, L, K,) and taken, (S, L,) from the enemy; (S, L, K;) pl. نَقَائِذُ: (S, L;) or the sing. of نقائذ, signifying horses saved, or rescued, or liberated, from the hands of men, or of the enemy, is 🡻نَقِيذٌ, without ة: (IAar, L:) and, accord. to El-Muffaddal, (L,) 🡻نَقِيذَةٌ signifies a coat of mail, دِرْعٌ; (L, K;) because it saves the person wearing it from the sword: (L:) and Az says, I have read in the handwriting of Shemir, that it signifies a coat of mail saved, or rescued, from the enemy. (L.) You say also, هُوَ نَقِيذَةُ بُؤْسٍ, and هُمْ نَقَائذُ بُؤْسٍ, He is saved, or rescued, from distress, or adversity; and they are &c. (A.) === مَا لَهُ شَقَذٌ وَلَا نَقَذٌ He possesses not anything. (K.) نَقِذَ 1 , aor. ﹷ, inf. n. نَقَذٌ; (Msb, K;) or نَقَذَ, (A, L,) aor. ﹹ, (L,) inf. n. نَقْذٌ; (A, L;) He became safe, in safety, saved, or liberated; he escaped. (A, L, Msb, K.) === نَقْذًا لَكَ Safety to thee! Said to a man stumbling. (A, K.) === See also 4. نَقِيذٌ and نَقِيذَةٌ: see نَقَذٌ. === Also the latter, A woman having had a husband. (K.) نَقَّذَ 2 see 4.
